summary: This project bridges the abstract elegance of network topology with the lived human experience of visual perception. It aligns with my interest in how mathematical structures underpin cultural and biological phenomena, specifically asking whether the model captures the narrative
body: Imagine a student tracing the veins on their own hand, marveling at the branching pattern, before we ask them to map the topology of the brain. There is a quiet beauty in trying to quantify the very structures that allow us to be fooled by sight. This proposal does not merely measure susceptibility; it invites us to consider the shape of perception itself. I am reminded of the work on topological data analysis in brain networks, where the shape of the data matters as much as the signal itself. Does the model account for the 'texture' of the illusion, or just the frequency of the error? If the network topology truly correlates with susceptibility, we are essentially mapping the geometry of the mind's vulnerability. Push forward, but keep the human story in the center of the equation.
